title: Quantum structure of the chiral vortical effect and boundary-induced vortical pumping
summary: The chiral vortical effect (CVE) -- an axial current driven by rotation in chiral matter -- appears in systems ranging from relativistic fluids to Weyl semimetals, yet its quantum origin remains unclear because existing derivations are semiclassical. We present an exact quantum solution of a rotating Weyl fermion in a finite cylinder. We show that the bulk vortical response is entirely a magnetization current while the current density on the rotation axis remains finite and matches semiclassical predictions. For spin-polarized boundary conditions, we uncover an additional effect beyond the known CVE: a robust family of chiral modes that transport axial charge, $ΔQ=χN^2\,Δθ/4π$, under rotation by angle $Δθ$, where $χ$ is the Weyl node chirality and $N$ is the number of chiral modes. The pump is independent of temperature, Fermi level and Weyl velocities, but depends on the UV-sensitive number $N$. These results establish a fully quantum picture of the CVE and reveal a boundary-enforced chiral spectral structure underlying vortical response in Weyl systems.

AI.tldr: This paper resolves the quantum origin of the chiral vortical effect (CVE) in Weyl fermion systems by providing an exact quantum solution in a finite cylinder geometry. It reveals that the semiclassical CVE is actually a magnetization current, and discovers a new boundary-driven chiral charge pump mechanism under rotation.
AI.motivation: The chiral vortical effect (CVE) - an axial current induced by rotation in chiral matter - appears across diverse systems from relativistic fluids to Weyl semimetals. However, its quantum origin remained unclear because all existing derivations were semiclassical. A fully quantum mechanical understanding was lacking.
AI.method: The authors solve the quantum mechanics of a rotating Weyl fermion exactly in a finite cylinder geometry, examining both bulk and boundary contributions under spin-polarized boundary conditions.
AI.result: The key findings are: (1) The bulk vortical response is entirely a magnetization current, not a transport current. (2) The current density on the rotation axis remains finite and matches semiclassical predictions. (3) For spin-polarized boundary conditions, a new effect beyond the known CVE is discovered: a robust family of chiral modes transporting axial charge ΔQ = χN²Δθ/4π under rotation by angle Δθ, where χ is Weyl node chirality and N is the number of chiral modes. This chiral charge pump is independent of temperature, Fermi level, and Weyl velocities, but depends on the UV-sensitive number N.
AI.conclusion: This work establishes a fully quantum picture of the CVE and reveals that the vortical response in Weyl systems has an underlying boundary-enforced chiral spectral structure. It bridges the gap between semiclassical intuition and exact quantum mechanics, showing that boundary conditions play a crucial role in generating the chiral transport phenomena.

title: Bond-density-wave orders induced by geometric frustration in the kagome metal CeRu3Si2
summary: Geometric frustration gives rise to vast manifolds of degenerate ground states and competing orders in spin and charge systems. Typically, classical ground states are governed by a local ``zero-sum constraint" that relieves frustrated antiferromagnetic interactions or Coulomb repulsion. To date, the paradigm of geometric frustration has yielded a rich landscape of emergent phases, from spin ices and quantum spin liquids to charge glasses. However, an analogous phase rooted in chemical bonding has yet to be firmly demonstrated. Here we report the discovery of bond-density-wave orders induced by geometric frustration in the kagome metal CeRu$_3$Si$_2$ above room temperature. Through synchrotron X-ray diffraction, real-space transmission electron microscopy, and model calculations, we observe two distinct long-period superlattices with harmonic and anharmonic structural modulations. Crucially, interlayer bonds between kagome planes modulate in a sublattice-selective manner to fulfill the zero-sum constraint on the kagome lattice. We demonstrate the potential of kagome metals to host complex bond-ordered states constrained by geometric frustration and establish chemical bonding as a distinct pathway to frustration physics in quantum materials even above room temperature.

AI.tldr: Researchers discovered bond-density-wave orders induced by geometric frustration in the kagome metal CeRu₃Si₂ above room temperature, demonstrating that chemical bonding provides a distinct pathway to frustration physics in quantum materials.
AI.motivation: While geometric frustration has been extensively studied in spin systems (spin ices, quantum spin liquids) and charge systems (charge glasses), an analogous phase rooted in chemical bonding had not been firmly demonstrated. The goal was to discover such a phase in a kagome metal.
AI.method: The researchers used synchrotron X-ray diffraction, real-space transmission electron microscopy, and model calculations to investigate the structural modulations in CeRu₃Si₂.
AI.result: Two distinct long-period superlattices were observed with harmonic and anharmonic structural modulations. Crucially, interlayer bonds between kagome planes modulate in a sublattice-selective manner to fulfill the zero-sum constraint on the kagome lattice.
AI.conclusion: This work establishes chemical bonding as a distinct pathway to frustration physics in quantum materials, demonstrating that kagome metals can host complex bond-ordered states constrained by geometric frustration even above room temperature. The discovery opens new avenues for exploring frustration-induced phases rooted in chemical bonding.

title: Sign-Free Evidence for a d-Wave Superfluid Stiffness Dome in the Doped Hubbard Model
summary: We construct an effective single-particle Hamiltonian $K_{\mathrm{eff}}$ from Monte Carlo--averaged matrix logarithms of the imaginary-time propagator in determinant quantum Monte Carlo (DQMC). The logarithm maps the multiplicative sign problem into an additive framework where the central limit theorem guarantees convergence, rendering $K_{\mathrm{eff}}$ sign-problem-free: both sign sectors yield identical dispersions to $<1\%$. $K_{\mathrm{eff}}$ captures the exact correlated single-particle spectrum, incorporating all self-energy effects non-perturbatively. Applied to the Hubbard model ($t'/t = -0.30$, $U/t = 4$), $K_{\mathrm{eff}}$ reveals a $d$-wave pseudogap with strong nodal-antinodal dichotomy below a computational phase transition at $T^*$. Three sign-free observables provide evidence consistent with spin-fluctuation pairing: (i) the gap ratio $R_g > 1$ confirms $d$-wave symmetry -- a temperature-independent property of the correlated band structure that provides the medium for pairing; (ii) the superfluid stiffness $ρ_s$ forms a dome across doping at $L = 8$, $10$, and $12$, exceeding the Berezinskii-Kosterlitz-Thouless threshold by $5$-$7\times$ at the dome peak; (iii) $S(π,π)$ is approximately flat across doping, establishing that the dome originates from Fermi-surface geometry responding to uniform spin-fluctuation glue. The pseudogap grows monotonically toward half-filling while $ρ_s$ forms a dome, mirroring cuprate phenomenology where $T_c$ is limited by the superfluid density (Uemura relation). Vertex corrections remain to be quantified.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a sign-problem-free method to extract the exact correlated single-particle Hamiltonian from determinant quantum Monte Carlo simulations. Applied to the Hubbard model, it reveals a d-wave pseudogap with nodal-antinodal dichotomy, a superfluid stiffness dome, and signatures of spin-fluctuation pairing, mirroring cuprate phenomenology.
AI.motivation: The sign problem in quantum Monte Carlo simulations limits access to the exact correlated single-particle spectrum. The authors aim to overcome this by mapping the multiplicative sign problem into an additive framework using matrix logarithms of the imaginary-time propagator.
AI.method: The authors construct an effective single-particle Hamiltonian K_eff from Monte Carlo-averaged matrix logarithms of the imaginary-time propagator in determinant quantum Monte Carlo (DQMC). This logarithm transforms the multiplicative sign problem into an additive one where the central limit theorem guarantees convergence, making K_eff sign-problem-free.
AI.result: Applied to the Hubbard model (t'/t = -0.30, U/t = 4), K_eff reveals: (1) A d-wave pseudogap with strong nodal-antinodal dichotomy below a computational phase transition at T*; (2) Gap ratio Rg > 1 confirming d-wave symmetry as a temperature-independent property; (3) Superfluid stiffness ρs forms a dome across doping at L = 8, 10, and 12, exceeding BKT threshold by 5-7× at peak; (4) S(π,π) is approximately flat across doping. The pseudogap grows toward half-filling while ρs forms a dome, mirroring cuprate phenomenology.
AI.conclusion: The sign-problem-free K_eff successfully captures the exact correlated single-particle spectrum non-perturbatively. The results provide evidence for spin-fluctuation pairing mechanism in the Hubbard model, with the superfluid stiffness dome and pseudogap behavior consistent with cuprate superconductivity phenomenology. Vertex corrections remain to be quantified.

title: Bond-Length-Driven Magnetic Transition in Quasi-One-Dimensional CrSb$X_3$ ($X$=S, Se)
summary: Using {\it ab initio} calculations, we investigate the magnetic ground states of quasi-one-dimensional insulating CrSb$X_3$ ($X$ = S, Se) with infinite double-rutile chains. Within conventional band theory, without explicit Coulomb correlations ($U$), we obtain band gaps in close agreement with experiment. Remarkably, we find that the magnetic order is highly sensitive to the Cr-Cr bond length $d_{\rm Cr-Cr}$: increasing the bond length induces a transition from antiferromagnetic to ferromagnetic order at a critical distance $d^c_{\rm Cr-Cr} \approx 3.53 (\pm 0.05)$ Å. Accordingly, CrSbS$_3$ lies near the transition boundary, whereas CrSbSe$_3$ is robustly ferromagnetic, in good agreement with experiment. Analysis of the exchange interactions reveals that the first-order phase transition is dominated by a sign reversal of the intrachain nearest-neighbor superexchange $J_1$ mediated by chalcogen ions, while the intrachain direct exchange $J_2$ remains ferromagnetic and changes only gradually. This behavior reflects an emergent Bethe-Slater-like behavior driven by competing exchange pathways in a quasi-1D transition-metal system, where the competition between $J_1$ and $J_2$ dictates the magnetic ground state. Besides, the electronic structures of the ground states of each compound are investigated.

AI.tldr: Using ab initio calculations, this paper investigates magnetic ground states of quasi-1D CrSbX3 (X=S, Se) compounds, discovering that magnetic order transitions from antiferromagnetic to ferromagnetic at a critical Cr-Cr bond length of ~3.53 Å, with CrSbS3 near the boundary and CrSbSe3 robustly ferromagnetic.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to understand the magnetic behavior of quasi-one-dimensional insulating CrSbX3 compounds with infinite double-rutile chains, particularly the sensitivity of magnetic order to Cr-Cr bond length and the underlying exchange mechanisms.
AI.method: The study employs ab initio calculations without explicit Coulomb correlations (U) within conventional band theory, analyzing exchange interactions and electronic structures of the ground states.
AI.result: A critical Cr-Cr bond length (~3.53 Å) was identified where magnetic order transitions from antiferromagnetic to ferromagnetic. CrSbS3 lies near the transition boundary while CrSbSe3 is robustly ferromagnetic. The transition is driven by sign reversal of intrachain nearest-neighbor superexchange J1, while intrachain direct exchange J2 remains ferromagnetic with gradual changes.
AI.conclusion: The findings reveal emergent Bethe-Slater-like behavior in quasi-1D transition-metal systems, where competition between superexchange (J1) and direct exchange (J2) dictates the magnetic ground state. This provides understanding of magnetic phase transitions in these compounds.

title: Enantiopurity-Controlled Magnetism in a Two-Dimensional Organic-Inorganic Material
summary: Extended solids that combine unpaired electron spin and structural chirality can host unconventional magnetic behaviors with potential for electronic technologies. A versatile strategy for creating chiral solids is incorporation of chiral organic molecules into inorganic crystals. However, such hybrid organic-inorganic materials have so far been examined through the lens of absolute chirality, leaving enantiomeric excess (ee) underexplored as a tuning parameter. Here, we report two-dimensional (2D) intercalation compounds with controllable ee produced by cation exchange of MnPS$_3$ with chiral organic molecules. We show that these materials' magnetism is determined by intercalant ee rather than absolute chirality. Moreover, low-ee materials display thermally activated dynamic magnetism absent from enantiopure analogs. These ee-dependent magnetic behaviors are explained by local ordering of Mn vacancies, directed by correlated vacancy-intercalant electrostatics and confined molecular packing. Together, these results demonstrate a distinctive tuning strategy for molecule-material hybrids and establish design principles for 2D chiral and magnetically dynamic materials.

AI.tldr: Researchers created 2D chiral magnetic materials by intercalating chiral organic molecules into MnPS3 through cation exchange, demonstrating that enantiomeric excess (ee) - rather than absolute chirality - controls magnetic properties. Low-ee materials show unique thermally activated dynamic magnetism absent in enantiopure materials, arising from Mn vacancy ordering influenced by vacancy-intercalant electrostatics and molecular packing.
AI.motivation: While extended solids combining unpaired electron spin and structural chirality show promise for unconventional magnetic behaviors with potential electronic applications, and while creating chiral solids via incorporation of chiral organic molecules into inorganic crystals is a versatile strategy, the field has focused primarily on absolute chirality. The role of enantiomeric excess (ee) as a tuning parameter remained unexplored, representing a significant gap in understanding how to rationally design chiral magnetic materials.
AI.method: The researchers employed cation exchange reactions to intercalate chiral organic molecules into the 2D inorganic material MnPS3, creating a series of 2D intercalation compounds with controllable enantiomeric excess. They systematically varied the ee of the chiral intercalants and characterized how this parameter influences the magnetic properties of the resulting materials.
AI.result: The key findings reveal that the magnetism in these 2D intercalation compounds is determined by the intercalant ee rather than absolute chirality. Critically, low-ee materials exhibit thermally activated dynamic magnetism that is completely absent in enantiopure analogs. These ee-dependent magnetic behaviors are attributed to local ordering of Mn vacancies, which is directed by correlated vacancy-intercalant electrostatics and confined molecular packing within the material structure.
AI.conclusion: This work establishes enantiomeric excess (ee) as a powerful tuning parameter for chiral 2D magnetic materials, distinct from absolute chirality. The finding that low-ee materials exhibit thermally activated dynamic magnetism (absent in enantiopure analogs) provides a new dimension for controlling material properties. The underlying mechanism involves Mn vacancy ordering directed by vacancy-intercalant electrostatics and confined molecular packing. This study provides design principles for creating 2D chiral magnets with tunable magnetic dynamics, opening pathways for electronic technologies.

title: Electronic-Structure Correlations Governing Superconductivity in Nb-Based High-Entropy Alloys
summary: Superconducting high-entropy alloys have recently emerged as a new platform for exploring superconductivity in highly disordered metallic systems and may offer advantages for applications requiring mechanical robustness and tolerance to extreme environments. Yet the mechanisms that govern their superconductivity, particularly the roles of lattice distortion and complex local order, both inherent to high-entropy alloys, remain unclear. The conventional valence-electron-concentration rule fails to reliably predict superconducting behavior, motivating a correlation analysis that links performance to electronic structure and lattice disorder. Here, we study a systematic series of niobium-based body-centered-cubic high-entropy alloys, from binary to quinary compositions, designed to investigate the electronic and structural effects and identify the dominant factors controlling superconductivity. Our experimental results reveal that the superconducting critical properties evolve non-monotonically with alloy complexity. Interestingly, alloys with greater lattice distortion can still achieve higher critical temperature and upper critical field. These observations are corroborated by first-principles and Eliashberg analyses, which identify the position of the niobium d-band relative to the Fermi level as the primary driver of electron-phonon coupling, critical temperature, and upper critical field, with lattice distortion serving as a secondary modifier that generally weakens coupling. We consolidate these findings into a detailed correlation map linking superconducting properties to electronic-structure fingerprints and vibrational signatures, establishing a mechanism-informed design strategy for superconducting high-entropy alloys with enhanced critical temperature and field.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates superconductivity mechanisms in niobium-based body-centered-cubic high-entropy alloys (HEAs) from binary to quinary compositions. Using first-principles and Eliashberg analyses, researchers found that the position of the niobium d-band relative to the Fermi level is the primary driver of electron-phonon coupling and superconducting properties, while lattice distortion serves as a secondary modifier that generally weakens coupling.
AI.motivation: The conventional valence-electron-concentration rule fails to reliably predict superconducting behavior in high-entropy alloys. The mechanisms governing superconductivity in these highly disordered metallic systems, particularly the roles of lattice distortion and complex local order, remain unclear, motivating a systematic investigation.
AI.method: The study examined a systematic series of niobium-based body-centered-cubic high-entropy alloys from binary to quinary compositions. Experimental results were combined with first-principles calculations and Eliashberg analyses to investigate electronic and structural effects and identify dominant factors controlling superconductivity.
AI.result: Superconducting critical properties evolve non-monotonically with alloy complexity. Interestingly, alloys with greater lattice distortion can still achieve higher critical temperature (Tc) and upper critical field (Hc2). The position of the niobium d-band relative to the Fermi level was identified as the primary driver of electron-phonon coupling, Tc, and Hc2, with lattice distortion serving as a secondary modifier that generally weakens coupling.
AI.conclusion: The findings establish a detailed correlation map linking superconducting properties to electronic-structure fingerprints and vibrational signatures, providing a mechanism-informed design strategy for developing superconducting high-entropy alloys with enhanced critical temperature and upper critical field.

title: Hydrodynamic Backflow for Easing the Fermion Sign in Finite-Temperature Electron Path Integral Simulations
summary: Some notable systems, such as room-temperature superconductors and materials for controlled nuclear fusion, require an accurate description of finite-temperature quantum matter. Stochastic path integral methods are finite-temperature and numerically exact, but scale poorly with system size due the notorious Fermion sign problem. To somewhat mitigate this, we use a hydrodynamical backflow coordinate transformation. Our first attempt was a continuous normalizing flow machine learning approach to determine the optimal parameters. We found this to reduce the error of the total energy, approximately, three times at medium sign severity. Numerical issues challenged training effectively. Thus, a semi-analytic approach was developed to estimate the optimal parameters. We do this by using a derived expression dependent on a Bosonic observable. Hence, the calculation of these values does not have a sign problem. The resulting backflow transformations reduce the problem by multiple orders of magnitude, specifically, in the case of a harmonically trapped, two-dimensional electron gas at finite-temperature. The total energy of the system agrees with previous, backflow untransformed, studies and we calculate energies for up to 32 electrons. The limiting factor is found to be, primarily, the $O(N^3)$ calculation of the Jacobian, stemming from the coordinate transformation of the backflow. A more thorough implementation may further improve this scaling. Otherwise, a pathway for simulating electron systems at currently unreachable regimes is obtained. Finally, as a specific practical use case in energy storage systems, the quantum capacitance for graphene quantum dot materials is calculated.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses the Fermion sign problem in finite-temperature quantum matter simulations using hydrodynamical backflow coordinate transformations. Two approaches are presented: a machine learning normalizing flow method (reduced energy error ~3x) and a superior semi-analytic approach using Bosonic observables that eliminates the sign problem. The method successfully simulates a 2D electron gas with up to 32 electrons, though O(N³) Jacobian computation remains the bottleneck.
AI.motivation: Simulating finite-temperature quantum matter is crucial for understanding room-temperature superconductors and controlled nuclear fusion materials. Stochastic path integral methods are finite-temperature and numerically exact but suffer from the Fermion sign problem, which scales poorly with system size.
AI.method: The authors employ a hydrodynamical backflow coordinate transformation to mitigate the sign problem. They first attempted a continuous normalizing flow machine learning approach to optimize transformation parameters, then developed a semi-analytic approach using a derived expression dependent on a Bosonic observable to calculate optimal parameters without a sign problem.
AI.result: The semi-analytic backflow transformation reduces the sign problem by multiple orders of magnitude. For a harmonically trapped 2D electron gas at finite temperature, results agree with previous backflow-untransformed studies. Energies were calculated for up to 32 electrons. The ML approach reduced total energy error by approximately 3x at medium sign severity. The main limitation is the O(N³) Jacobian computation from the coordinate transformation.
AI.conclusion: The semi-analytic approach provides a viable pathway for simulating electron systems at previously unreachable regimes. As a practical application, quantum capacitance was calculated for graphene quantum dot materials. Future improvements in Jacobian calculation scaling could further extend the method's applicability.

title: Revealing Strain and Disorder in Transition-Metal Dichalcogenides Using Hyperspectral Photoluminescence Imaging
summary: Hyperspectral photoluminescence (HSPL) imaging provides spatially resolved spectral information for monolayer transition-metal dichalcogenides (TMDs), enabling the detection of subtle variations in excitonic features that are not accessible with conventional optical or photoluminescence intensity imaging. We employ HSPL to map the microscopic spatial distribution of strain and disorder in hBN-encapsulated MoSe$_2$ and WSe$_2$ samples. Quantitative extraction of exciton, trion, and biexciton energies and linewidths reveals strain gradients and localized deformations, such as wrinkles and ripples. The technique allows for characterization of regions with uniform optical properties and identification of areas affected by micro-scale disorder, which may be missed by optical microscopy. Measurements on samples with different device architectures and fabrication processes demonstrate the general utility of hyperspectral PL imaging for assessing spatial heterogeneity and optoelectronic quality in two-dimensional materials.

AI.tldr: Hyperspectral photoluminescence imaging enables spatially resolved spectral analysis of monolayer TMDs, revealing strain and disorder distributions that conventional imaging misses, with applications in evaluating 2D material quality.
AI.motivation: Conventional optical or photoluminescence intensity imaging cannot detect subtle variations in excitonic features in monolayer transition-metal dichalcogenides (TMDs), limiting the ability to assess spatial heterogeneity and optoelectronic quality in 2D materials.
AI.method: Hyperspectral photoluminescence (HSPL) imaging is employed to map the microscopic spatial distribution of strain and disorder in hBN-encapsulated MoSe₂ and WSe₂ samples, quantitatively extracting exciton, trion, and biexciton energies and linewidths.
AI.result: The technique reveals strain gradients and localized deformations (wrinkles and ripples), characterizes regions with uniform optical properties, and identifies areas affected by micro-scale disorder.
AI.conclusion: HSPL imaging demonstrates general utility for assessing spatial heterogeneity and optoelectronic quality in 2D materials across different device architectures and fabrication processes.

title: Chiral Superconductivity in Periodically Driven Altermagnet/Superconductor Heterostructures
summary: The interplay between magnetism and superconductivity provides a fertile ground for engineering exotic topological phases, while dynamical control via periodic driving offers a unique avenue to access quantum states that are inaccessible in static equilibrium. Here, we propose a strategy to achieve the Floquet chiral topological superconductivity in an altermagnet-superconductor heterostructure driven by elliptically polarized light. We show that for $s$-wave pairing, the system undergoes a transition from a trivial to a chiral topological superconducting phase. More strikingly, with the introduction of mixed $s+d$-wave pairing, we find that the system can access Floquet chiral topological superconducting phases with highly tunable Chern numbers up to N=4. These exotic phases are attributed to the intertwining of altermagnetism, superconducting pairing, and the periodic driving field. Our work establishes the light-driven altermagnetic heterostructure as a versatile platform for exploring and manipulating high-Chern-number chiral topological superconductivity.

AI.tldr: Proposes strategy to achieve Floquet chiral topological superconductivity in altermagnet-superconductor heterostructure driven by elliptically polarized light, achieving Chern numbers up to N=4 with mixed s+d-wave pairing.
AI.motivation: To achieve dynamical control of topological superconductivity via periodic driving, accessing quantum states that are inaccessible in static equilibrium, and to explore the interplay between magnetism and superconductivity for engineering exotic topological phases.
AI.method: Using elliptically polarized light to drive an altermagnet-superconductor heterostructure, with both s-wave and mixed s+d-wave pairing scenarios investigated.
AI.result: For s-wave pairing: system undergoes transition from trivial to chiral topological superconducting phase. For mixed s+d-wave pairing: system can access Floquet chiral topological superconducting phases with highly tunable Chern numbers up to N=4.
AI.conclusion: The work establishes light-driven altermagnetic heterostructure as a versatile platform for exploring and manipulating high-Chern-number chiral topological superconductivity, opening new avenues for engineering exotic topological phases through the interplay of altermagnetism, superconductivity, and periodic driving.

title: Moiré Mott correlated mosaics in twisted bilayer 1T-TaS$_2$
summary: The tunability and twist engineering of van der Waals materials enable the emergence of electronic states not present in individual monolayers. Among them, monolayer 1T-TaS$_2$ is a well-known Mott insulating system, whose star-of-David charge density wave reconstruction realizes an emergent triangular lattice of local magnetic moments. Interestingly, in its bulk form, the insulating gap is not correlation-driven, but stems from interlayer coupling. Here, we exploit the stacking-dependent nature of the insulating gap to show that in twisted 1T-TaS$_2$ bilayers, the spatially dependent competition between many-body and single-particle gaps creates Mott-trivial mosaic superlattices, featuring regions with local magnetic moments and non-magnetic insulating regions. We further demonstrate the tunability of the mosaic correlated state with an interlayer bias, giving rise to controllable charge transfer and quenching of correlations. Our results establish twisted 1T-TaS$_2$ as a flexible platform to engineer mixed spatially modulated correlated insulating phases, arising from the moiré profile.

AI.tldr: Twisted 1T-TaS2 bilayers exhibit spatially modulated correlated insulating phases where many-body Mott states and trivial insulating regions coexist in a mosaic superlattice pattern, tunable through interlayer bias.
AI.motivation: To explore how tunability and twist engineering in van der Waals materials can create electronic states not present in individual monolayers, specifically investigating the stacking-dependent insulating gap in 1T-TaS2
AI.method: Exploited the stacking-dependent nature of the insulating gap in twisted 1T-TaS2 bilayers to study the competition between many-body and single-particle gaps
AI.result: Discovery of Mott-trivial mosaic superlattices in twisted 1T-TaS2 bilayers, featuring regions with local magnetic moments coexisting with non-magnetic insulating regions, controllable via interlayer bias for charge transfer and correlation quenching
AI.conclusion: This work establishes twisted 1T-TaS2 as a platform for engineering spatially modulated correlated insulating phases, with potential for tunable mixed states through interlayer bias control.

title: Insulator-to-Metal Transitions Driven by Quantized Formal Polarization Mismatch
summary: We propose a mechanism for insulator-to-metal (IM) transitions driven by the mismatch of quantized formal polarization (QFP), a symmetry-protected bulk invariant. For a material with a low-symmetry insulating phase and a high-symmetry phase that allow distinct QFPs, any continuous path connecting them while preserving the symmetry of the low-symmetry phase must inevitably pass through an IM transition. The reason is that QFP remains invariant along any gapped symmetry-preserving evolution, whereas the high-symmetry phase requires a different QFP, which can only be accommodated by gap closing. First-principles calculations on two representative systems, two-dimensional InPS$_3$ and three-dimensional CdBiO$_3$, confirm this mechanism. Our results establish QFP mismatch as a general symmetry constraint on phase evolution and reveal a new route to symmetry-driven IM transitions in high-symmetry materials.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a new mechanism for insulator-to-metal (IM) transitions driven by mismatched quantized formal polarization (QFP), a symmetry-protected bulk invariant. When a low-symmetry insulating phase and a high-symmetry phase have distinct QFP values, any continuous path connecting them while preserving the low-symmetry phase's symmetry must necessarily pass through an IM transition, as QFP remains invariant along gapped paths but the high-symmetry phase requires a different QFP value that can only be achieved through gap closing.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to establish a general symmetry-based constraint that explains how and why insulator-to-metal transitions occur in certain materials, particularly those transitioning from low-symmetry to high-symmetry phases. The authors seek to identify a fundamental mechanism beyond traditional band theory explanations.
AI.method: The researchers used first-principles calculations on two representative systems: two-dimensional InPS₃ and three-dimensional CdBiO₃. They analyzed the relationship between quantized formal polarization (QFP) values and the symmetry constraints on continuous phase evolution paths.
AI.result: The mechanism was confirmed in both representative systems (2D InPS₃ and 3D CdBiO₃). The results demonstrate that QFP mismatch creates an unavoidable requirement for gap closing when attempting to connect phases with different QFP values while preserving symmetry.
AI.conclusion: The study establishes QFP mismatch as a general symmetry constraint on phase evolution and reveals a new route to symmetry-driven IM transitions in high-symmetry materials. This provides a framework for understanding and predicting IM transitions based on topological invariants rather than purely band structure considerations.

title: Dissecting superconductivity in the Ruddlesden-Popper nickelates: The role of electron correlation and interlayer magnetic exchange
summary: The discovery of superconductivity in the Ruddlesden-Popper (RP) nickelates has opened a new chapter in the search for high superconducting transition temperatures ($T_\mathrm{c}$) materials. A central and puzzling feature of this family is the wide variation in $T_\mathrm{c}$ despite their common NiO$_2$ building blocks, as highlighted by the recent observation of superconductivity at $\sim$ 30 K in trilayer $\mathrm{La_4Ni_3O_{10}}$, significantly lower than 80 K reported in bilayer $\mathrm{La_3Ni_2O_7}$. Understanding the factors that control $T_\mathrm{c}$ in this family is therefore of paramount importance. Here, we use resonant inelastic x-ray scattering (RIXS) to investigate the electronic and magnetic excitations of $\mathrm{La_4Ni_3O_{10}}$ in direct comparison with its bilayer counterpart. Our results reveal a markedly different landscape. $\mathrm{La_4Ni_3O_{10}}$ exhibits a more itinerant character, evidenced by broader Ni $dd$ orbital excitations and a strong Ni 3$d$ fluorescence continuum, suggesting weaker electronic correlations than in the bilayer. Despite this, well-defined collective spin excitations persist, including dispersive acoustic and optical magnon branches alongside an incommensurate spin density wave. Using linear spin wave theory, we extract the interlayer superexchange interaction ($J_z$) to be $\sim$ 22 meV, much smaller than that in $\mathrm{La_3Ni_2O_7}$. The weaker correlation and reduced interlayer exchange together provide a consistent explanation for the substantially lower $T_\mathrm{c}$ in the trilayer compound. Our findings establish interlayer magnetic coupling and electronic correlation as key parameters governing superconductivity in layered nickelates and offer critical constraints for understanding the pairing mechanism in this emerging family.

AI.tldr: This study investigates why trilayer La4Ni3O10 has much lower Tc (~30K) compared to bilayer La3Ni2O7 (~80K) in Ruddlesden-Popper nickelates, using RIXS to reveal that weaker electronic correlations and reduced interlayer magnetic coupling explain the lower Tc.
AI.motivation: The wide variation in Tc despite common NiO2 building blocks in RP nickelates is puzzling and understanding Tc control factors is crucial for advancing high-Tc superconductivity research.
AI.method: Resonant inelastic x-ray scattering (RIXS) was used to investigate electronic and magnetic excitations of La4Ni3O10 in direct comparison with bilayer La3Ni2O7, including analysis of Ni dd orbital excitations, Ni 3d fluorescence continuum, and spin excitations using linear spin wave theory.
AI.result: La4Ni3O10 exhibits more itinerant character with broader Ni dd orbital excitations and strong Ni 3d fluorescence continuum, indicating weaker electronic correlations than the bilayer. Well-defined collective spin excitations persist, including dispersive acoustic and optical magnon branches and an incommensurate spin density wave. The interlayer superexchange interaction Jz is ~22 meV, much smaller than in La3Ni2O7.
AI.conclusion: Weaker electronic correlations and reduced interlayer exchange together provide a consistent explanation for substantially lower Tc in the trilayer compound. Interlayer magnetic coupling and electronic correlation are key parameters governing superconductivity in layered nickelates, offering critical constraints for understanding the pairing mechanism.

title: Suppression of the tendency toward antiferromagnetic order in the Dirac semimetal SrIrO$_3$
summary: The entangled charge and spin dynamics in strongly electron correlated system has been a fruitful playground for exploring new physical phenomena. Here with resonant inelastic X-ray scattering we studied the spin dynamics of SrIrO$_3$, a half-filled paramagnetic semimetal hosting highly itinerant Dirac Fermions due to its topological band structure. Our results show that its magnetic excitations share much similarity to the ordered compounds upon Sn substitution in exchange strength and AFM instability, while the system maintains spin non-ordered. Further, the non-ordered pristine SrIrO$_3$ hosts even longer lifetime magnetic excitations near the AFM zone center comparing to the Sn substituted ordered compounds, contrary to general expectation. These observations indicate an interesting connection between band topology and electron correlation in SrIrO$_3$.

AI.tldr: This paper uses resonant inelastic X-ray scattering (RIXS) to study spin dynamics in SrIrO3, a half-filled paramagnetic semimetal with itinerant Dirac fermions and topological band structure. The study reveals magnetic excitations similar to ordered Sn-substituted compounds in exchange strength and AFM instability, but with longer lifetime excitations in the non-ordered pristine system.
AI.motivation: The study aims to explore the entangled charge and spin dynamics in strongly electron correlated systems, specifically investigating the connection between band topology and electron correlation in SrIrO3.
AI.method: Resonant inelastic X-ray scattering (RIXS) was employed to study the spin dynamics of SrIrO3.
AI.result: The magnetic excitations in pristine SrIrO3 share similarities with Sn-substituted ordered compounds in exchange strength and AFM instability, while maintaining a spin non-ordered state. Surprisingly, the non-ordered pristine system exhibits longer lifetime magnetic excitations near the AFM zone center compared to the Sn-substituted ordered compounds.
AI.conclusion: The observations indicate an interesting connection between band topology and electron correlation in SrIrO3, suggesting that the topological band structure influences spin dynamics even in the absence of magnetic ordering.

title: Precipitate-Induced Dynamic Strain Aging and Its Effect on the Strain Rate Sensitivity of Precipitation Hardened Aluminum Alloys
summary: We examine precipitate-induced dynamic strain aging in precipitation-hardened Al-Cu alloys by combining atomistic simulations, kinetic Monte Carlo, and analytical rate theory. Atomistic simulations were used to characterize (1) the energetics of nearest neighbour Cu<->Al exchanges at dislocation - precipitate junctions and (2) the subsequent change in obstacle strength. For robustness, the simulations were performed with two distinct interatomic potentials. The resulting catalog of local Cu-Al exchange events was used as input for a kinetic Monte Carlo model of the time-dependent evolution of obstacle strength during dislocation pinning at the precipitate. The predicted strengthening kinetics were then embedded in an analytical dynamic strain aging model to predict the strain-rate sensitivity parameter. On the whole, the modeling predicts a low strain-rate sensitivity across a broad range of intermediate quasi-static strain rates, consistent with experimental observations for precipitate-strengthened alloys. The results therefore identify a mechanistic origin of the low strain-rate sensitivity in precipitation hardened aluminum alloys, emerging directly from the kinetics of dislocation-precipitate interactions when nearest neighbour Cu<->Al exchanges are considered.

AI.tldr: This study investigates precipitate-induced dynamic strain aging in precipitation-hardened Al-Cu alloys using atomistic simulations, kinetic Monte Carlo, and analytical rate theory. The research characterizes Cu<->Al exchange events at dislocation-precipitate junctions and predicts low strain-rate sensitivity across quasi-static strain rates, consistent with experimental observations.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to understand the mechanistic origin of the low strain-rate sensitivity observed in precipitation hardened aluminum alloys, which is not well understood despite its importance for material properties.
AI.method: The study combines three approaches: (1) atomistic simulations with two interatomic potentials to characterize energetics of nearest neighbor Cu<->Al exchanges at dislocation-precipitate junctions and subsequent changes in obstacle strength; (2) kinetic Monte Carlo modeling of time-dependent evolution of obstacle strength during dislocation pinning; (3) analytical dynamic strain aging model to predict strain-rate sensitivity parameter.
AI.result: The modeling predicts low strain-rate sensitivity across a broad range of intermediate quasi-static strain rates, consistent with experimental observations for precipitate-strengthened alloys.
AI.conclusion: The results identify a mechanistic origin of low strain-rate sensitivity in precipitation hardened aluminum alloys, emerging directly from the kinetics of dislocation-precipitate interactions when nearest neighbor Cu<->Al exchanges are considered.

title: Jahn-Teller distortion on strained La$_3$Ni$_2$O$_7$ thin films
summary: We present a systematic study of the electronic structure of strained La$_3$Ni$_2$O$_7$ thin films. We show that biaxial compressive strain mainly elongates the outer apical Ni-O bond while leaving the inner apical Ni-O bond nearly unchanged. As a result, the Jahn-Teller splitting $Δ_{JT}$ is strongly enhanced, whereas the interlayer $d_{z^2}$ hopping $t_\perp^z$ changes only weakly. Since superconductivity is widely believed to emerge only below a critical in-plane lattice constant, our results identify the strain-enhanced $Δ_{JT}$ as the relevant microscopic tuning parameter. Consistently, the calculated Fermi surfaces and Hall response for LaAlO$_3$ and SrLaAlO$_4$ substrates agree with ARPES and Hall measurements. Our results identify Jahn-Teller distortion as a key tuning parameter in strained La$_3$Ni$_2$O$_7$ and support its central role in optimizing superconductivity in bilayer nickelates.

AI.tldr: This study investigates strained La₃Ni₂O₇ thin films and identifies strain-enhanced Jahn-Teller distortion as the key microscopic parameter for tuning superconductivity in bilayer nickelates, rather than interlayer hopping.
AI.motivation: Superconductivity in bilayer nickelates like La₃Ni₂O₇ is believed to emerge only below a critical in-plane lattice constant, but the underlying microscopic tuning parameter remained unclear. This study aims to identify what actually controls superconductivity in this material under strain.
AI.method: Systematic study of electronic structure in strained La₃Ni₂O₇ thin films using theoretical calculations, comparing results for LaAlO₃ and SrLaAlO₄ substrates with experimental ARPES and Hall measurements.
AI.result: Biaxial compressive strain primarily elongates the outer apical Ni-O bond while leaving the inner apical bond nearly unchanged. This strongly enhances Jahn-Teller splitting (Δ_JT) while only weakly affecting interlayer d_z² hopping (t_⊥^z). The calculated Fermi surfaces and Hall response agree with ARPES and Hall measurements.
AI.conclusion: Jahn-Teller distortion, not interlayer hopping, is the key microscopic tuning parameter for optimizing superconductivity in bilayer nickelates. Strain-enhanced Δ_JT explains the strain dependence of superconductivity in La₃Ni₂O₇ thin films.

title: Entropic crystallization of geometrically frustrated magnets on 1/1 approximant Tsai-type quasicrystal
summary: We have studied the antiferromagnetic Ising model on the icosahedral bcc lattice, as a model system of 1/1 approximant Tsai-type quasicrystals. We addressed thermal equilibrium properties of this system with Markov-chain Monte Carlo simulation supplemented with the parallel tempering technique to accelerate the relaxation dynamics. As a result, we found a second-order phase transition takes place to the magnetic ordered phase with ${\mathbb Z_3}\times {\mathbb Z_2}$ symmetry breaking. Despite the ordering, the low-temperature phase keeps macroscopic degeneracy as identified by finite residual entropy, $\mathcal{S}\sim0.1767/{\rm spin}$. Remarkably, the existence of residual entropy turns out to play a major role in the formation of magnetic order. Generation of domain wall is suppressed, as it reduces the residual entropy locally stored in icosahedra, beyond the gain of configurational entropy due to domain wall patterns. Magnetic order arises out of this competition as entropic crystallization, which manifest universal mechanism of strongly frustrated systems with large geometrical units.

AI.tldr: The study investigates the antiferromagnetic Ising model on the icosahedral bcc lattice (a model for Tsai-type quasicrystals) using Monte Carlo simulations with parallel tempering. It discovers a second-order phase transition with Z3 × Z2 symmetry breaking, revealing that residual entropy (~0.1767/spin) drives magnetic ordering through an "entropic crystallization" mechanism.
AI.motivation: To understand thermal equilibrium properties of frustrated magnetic systems modeling 1/1 approximant Tsai-type quasicrystals, particularly the role of large geometrical units and macroscopic degeneracy in phase transitions.
AI.method: Markov-chain Monte Carlo simulation supplemented with parallel tempering technique to accelerate relaxation dynamics.
AI.result: A second-order phase transition occurs to a magnetically ordered phase with Z3 × Z2 symmetry breaking. The low-temperature phase retains macroscopic degeneracy with finite residual entropy S ~ 0.1767/spin. Domain wall generation is suppressed because it reduces locally stored residual entropy in icosahedra beyond the configurational entropy gain from domain wall patterns.
AI.conclusion: The residual entropy plays a major role in magnetic order formation through "entropic crystallization." This reveals a universal mechanism for strongly frustrated systems with large geometrical units, where competing entropic effects drive ordering rather than energetic preferences.

title: Magnetoelectric Coupling in Nickel-Cobalt Ferrite and Lanthanum Ferrite Heterostructure Composites: Experimental Evidence and Simulation-Driven Insights
summary: A material that reveals two or more ferroelectric properties at the same time is called multiferroic materials. The most commonly multiferroic materials shows ferroelectricity and ferromagnetism property within a single phase. Accordingly such materials can possess both spontaneous magnetization and electric polarization and which can be individually controlled through external electric or magnetic fields. The unique coexistence of ferroelectric properties opens up possibilities for innovative applications, including memory devices, sensors, and actuators that are responsive to both electrical and magneticstimuli. Multiferroics gives a valuable foundation for generating cutting edge multifunctional devices, exhibiting their versatility across broad area technological fields. Applications include sensors, transducers, spintronics, terahertz emitters, miniature antennas, energy harvesting, multiple state memory storage, electric field controlled ferromagnetic resonance devices, and nanoscale electronics. Researchers are continually work to discovering new materials exploring the fundamental mechanisms involved, and optimizing their performance for various application. Multiferroic materials has a promising area for innovation and exploration the field of advance technologies this type of materials contributing not only to device development but also enhancing the understanding of the interactions between ferroelectric and magnetic orders. Additionally, magnetoelectric composites is a type of multifunctional material showing strong coupling between magnetic and electric properties for further technological applications. Magnetoelectric (ME) composites are synthesis by combining separate magnetic and electric materials to produce unique functionalities through their interactions. This coupling enables the control of magnetic properties by an electric field and vice versa.

AI.tldr: This paper reviews multiferroic materials that exhibit multiple ferroelectric properties (typically ferroelectricity and ferromagnetism) simultaneously within a single phase, enabling control of both spontaneous magnetization and electric polarization through external fields. It also discusses magnetoelectric composites that combine magnetic and electric materials to achieve strong coupling between their properties.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to explore multiferroic materials for innovative multifunctional applications in memory devices, sensors, actuators, spintronics, and other advanced technologies, while advancing understanding of interactions between ferroelectric and magnetic orders.
AI.method: This appears to be a review paper that discusses the properties, applications, and mechanisms of multiferroic materials and magnetoelectric composites without describing a specific experimental methodology.
AI.result: Multiferroic materials demonstrate promising applications across multiple technological fields including sensors, transducers, spintronics, terahertz emitters, energy harvesting, and multiple state memory storage. Magnetoelectric composites show strong coupling between magnetic and electric properties, enabling bidirectional control between magnetic and electric fields.
AI.conclusion: Multiferroic materials represent a promising area for innovation in advanced technologies, offering both device development opportunities and fundamental insights into the interactions between ferroelectric and magnetic orders. Continued research in discovering new materials and optimizing their performance remains essential for technological advancement.

title: Chiral skyrmionic superconductivity from doping a Chern Ferromagnet
summary: We show that chiral superconductivity can be stabilized by hole doping a Chern ferromagnet. Performing exact diagonalization and density-matrix-renormalization-group calculations on the repulsive Kane-Mele-Hubbard model at hole doping relative to filling $ν=1$ electron per unit cell, we find that a Cooper pair formed by a magnon (spin-flip excitation) bound to two holes is stabilized at sufficiently strong interactions and sufficiently large Ising spin-orbit coupling (SOC). This Cooper pair exhibits both finite spin chirality -- signaling a noncoplanar skyrmionic spin texture -- and chiral $f$-wave symmetry. The pairing and spin chirality are set by the Chern number/polarization of the parent Chern ferromagnet. We further find that interactions between skyrmion Cooper pairs evolve from repulsive to attractive as the Ising SOC increases, revealing an intermediate-SOC region where chiral superconductivity can emerge from the condensation of hole-skyrmion Cooper pairs. Our findings provide a novel microscopic mechanism for chiral superconductivity and may be relevant for the recent observation of superconductivity in the MoTe$_2$ moiré superlattice.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates that chiral superconductivity can emerge from hole-doped Chern ferromagnets via a novel mechanism where Cooper pairs form from magnons bound to two holes (skyrmion Cooper pairs).
AI.motivation: The motivation is to understand and establish a microscopic mechanism for chiral superconductivity, which has been experimentally observed but lacks clear theoretical explanation.
AI.method: The authors employ exact diagonalization and density-matrix-renormalization-group (DMRG) calculations on the repulsive Kane-Mele-Hubbard model at hole doping relative to filling ν=1 electron per unit cell.
AI.result: The key finding is the stabilization of a Cooper pair formed by a magnon (spin-flip excitation) bound to two holes, exhibiting finite spin chirality indicating a noncoplanar skyrmionic spin texture, and chiral f-wave symmetry. The pairing and spin chirality are determined by the Chern number/polarization of the parent Chern ferromagnet. Additionally, interactions between skyrmion Cooper pairs transition from repulsive to attractive as the Ising spin-orbit coupling increases.
AI.conclusion: This work provides a novel microscopic mechanism for chiral superconductivity and may be relevant to the recently observed superconductivity in MoTe2 moiré superlattices.

title: Detecting Symmetry-Resolved Entanglement: A Quantum Monte Carlo Approach
summary: Symmetry and entanglement are two fundamental concepts in quantum many-body physics. Their interplay is captured by symmetry-resolved entanglement, which decomposes the total entanglement into contributions from different symmetry sectors. Computing symmetry-resolved entanglement in strongly interacting higher-dimensional quantum systems remains challenging. Here, we introduce a quantum Monte Carlo (QMC) approach for computing symmetry-resolved Rényi entropies (SRRE) in large-scale interacting systems by measuring disorder (symmetry-twisted) operators on replica manifolds and reconstructing SRRE from the corresponding charged moments. We apply this method to the transverse-field Ising model (TFIM) in one and two dimensions. In one dimension, we recover the conformal-field-theory prediction for the logarithmic scaling of the disorder operator and observe the expected approach to entanglement equipartition. In two dimensions, our data provide numerical evidence consistent with entanglement equipartition at the (2+1)D Ising critical point. We further apply the framework to the 1D Heisenberg chain and obtain results consistent with the expected asymptotic scaling and finite-size corrections in the U(1)-resolved sectors. Our work establishes a practical numerical route to symmetry-resolved entanglement in interacting lattice models and provides a useful framework for future studies beyond one dimension.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a Quantum Monte Carlo method for computing symmetry-resolved Rényi entropies in strongly interacting quantum many-body systems by measuring disorder operators on replica manifolds, applied to the transverse-field Ising model in 1D/2D and the Heisenberg chain.
AI.motivation: Computing symmetry-resolved entanglement in strongly interacting higher-dimensional quantum systems remains challenging, despite being important for understanding the interplay between symmetry and entanglement in quantum many-body physics.
AI.method: A quantum Monte Carlo (QMC) approach is proposed to compute symmetry-resolved Rényi entropies (SRRE) by measuring disorder (symmetry-twisted) operators on replica manifolds and reconstructing SRRE from the corresponding charged moments. This method is applied to the transverse-field Ising model (1D and 2D) and the 1D Heisenberg chain.
AI.result: In 1D TFIM, the method recovers the conformal-field-theory prediction for logarithmic scaling of the disorder operator and shows expected approach to entanglement equipartition. In 2D TFIM at the (2+1)D Ising critical point, numerical evidence consistent with entanglement equipartition is obtained. For the 1D Heisenberg chain, results are consistent with expected asymptotic scaling and finite-size corrections in the U(1)-resolved sectors.
AI.conclusion: The proposed QMC method provides a practical numerical route to symmetry-resolved entanglement in interacting lattice models, establishing a useful framework for future studies beyond one dimension.

title: Abrupt crystallization from shock-compressed CaSiO3 glass
summary: We have performed in situ time-resolved X-ray diffraction at ~100 GPa on laser-shocked CaSiO3 glass to investigate the glass-to-crystal transition. At this extreme pressure, we observe the ultrafast crystallization of the CaSiO3 perovskite structure from the compressed amorphous phase, with a typical nucleation time of 1.69 +/- 0.10 ns and a final grainsize of ~20 nm. The grain size temporal evolution suggest a diffusion controlled transformation. Moreover, the observed concomitant explosive grain growth together with the release wave arrival into shocked CaSiO3 also suggests a role of the release in the nucleation process.

AI.tldr: This study investigates the ultrafast crystallization of CaSiO3 glass under extreme pressure (~100 GPa) using laser shock and time-resolved X-ray diffraction, revealing a glass-to-perovskite transition with nanometer-scale grain formation.
AI.motivation: Understanding phase transitions of materials under extreme high-pressure conditions is crucial for geophysics and materials science, but the kinetics of glass-to-crystal transformations at pressures exceeding 100 GPa remain poorly understood due to experimental challenges.
AI.method: The researchers used in situ time-resolved X-ray diffraction at ~100 GPa on laser-shocked CaSiO3 glass to observe the crystallization process in real time, measuring nucleation times and grain size evolution.
AI.result: The study observed ultrafast crystallization of CaSiO3 perovskite structure with a nucleation time of 1.69 ± 0.10 ns and final grain size of ~20 nm. The transformation appears diffusion-controlled, and the release wave arrival plays a role in the nucleation process.
AI.conclusion: This work demonstrates that under extreme pressure conditions, CaSiO3 glass can rapidly crystallize into perovskite structure with nanocrystalline grains, and the release wave plays a key role in triggering nucleation.

title: Quantum-Information Measure of Electron Localization
summary: Understanding electron localization in molecules and materials plays a central role in electronic structure theory, and will increase in importance with the rise of data driven approaches. The electron localization function (ELF) is widely used to visualize electron organization in molecules and materials, and it remains a central ingredient in modern density functional approximations. Yet its formulation retains highly empirical elements. Here we introduce a fully non empirical measure of electron localization derived from the concurrence of a correlated two spin mixed state. This construction yields a genuine two point localization indicator grounded in quantum information theory, removing the ad hoc steps underlying the ELF. We show that atomic shells, covalent and ionic bonds, lone pairs, molecular dissociation, and charge transfer processes are captured. The method is straightforward to evaluate numerically.

AI.tldr: A new fully non-empirical measure of electron localization based on quantum information theory (concurrence of correlated two spin mixed state) is introduced as an alternative to the empirical electron localization function (ELF), successfully capturing atomic shells, bonds, lone pairs, dissociation, and charge transfer.
AI.motivation: Understanding electron localization is crucial for electronic structure theory and is becoming increasingly important with data-driven approaches. The electron localization function (ELF), a widely used tool for visualizing electron organization, retains highly empirical elements in its formulation. This limitation motivates the development of a more rigorous, non-empirical approach.
AI.method: The paper introduces a fully non-empirical measure of electron localization derived from the concurrence of a correlated two spin mixed state. This quantum information theory-based construction yields a genuine two-point localization indicator that removes the ad hoc steps underlying the ELF.
AI.result: The method successfully captures atomic shells, covalent and ionic bonds, lone pairs, molecular dissociation, and charge transfer processes. It provides a straightforward numerical evaluation while being grounded in quantum information theory rather than empirical parameters.
AI.conclusion: This work presents a principled alternative to the empirical ELF, offering a fully non-empirical localization indicator based on quantum information theory that maintains predictive capability across diverse chemical scenarios while eliminating arbitrary elements in the formulation.

title: Machine Learning Interatomic Potentials for Million-Atom Simulations of Multicomponent Alloys
summary: Machine learning interatomic potentials (MLIPs) with broad chemical flexibility are important for atomistic simulations of compositionally complex materials such as high-entropy alloys. Here, we study two state-of-the-art MLIP frameworks, the neuroevolution potential (NEP) and the graph atomic cluster expansion (GRACE), for 16 elemental metals and multicomponent alloys. GRACE potential with Finnis-Sinclair type shows substantially higher training efficiency and consistently, though only slightly, better accuracy for mechanical properties, thermal stability, and chemical extrapolation. In contrast, NEP achieves an approximately 60-fold higher inference speed, making it attractive for million-atom molecular dynamics simulations. We further examine uncertainty quantification strategies and find that ensemble-based uncertainty correlates robustly with model error, whereas D-optimality is less reliable for the systems considered here. Large-scale nonequilibrium molecular dynamics simulations of shock propagation further show that NEP, combined with ensemble-based uncertainty quantification, enables efficient and reliable simulations under extreme dynamic conditions.

AI.tldr: This paper compares two MLIP frameworks (NEP and GRACE) for atomistic simulations of 16 elemental metals and high-entropy alloys. GRACE shows slightly better accuracy and training efficiency, while NEP achieves ~60-fold faster inference speed. Ensemble-based uncertainty quantification is more reliable than D-optimality, and NEP combined with ensemble uncertainty enables efficient large-scale shock simulations.
AI.motivation: Machine learning interatomic potentials (MLIPs) with broad chemical flexibility are needed for atomistic simulations of compositionally complex materials such as high-entropy alloys, which are challenging to model accurately.
AI.method: The authors compared NEP and GRACE frameworks across 16 elemental metals and multicomponent alloys. They evaluated training efficiency, accuracy for mechanical properties and thermal stability, chemical extrapolation capability, inference speed, uncertainty quantification strategies (ensemble-based vs D-optimality), and shock propagation simulations via nonequilibrium MD.
AI.result: GRACE with Finnis-Sinclair type showed substantially higher training efficiency and consistently (though only slightly) better accuracy for mechanical properties, thermal stability, and chemical extrapolation. In contrast, NEP achieved approximately 60-fold higher inference speed, making it suitable for million-atom molecular dynamics. Ensemble-based uncertainty quantification correlated robustly with model error, while D-optimality was less reliable. NEP combined with ensemble-based uncertainty enabled efficient and reliable shock propagation simulations under extreme dynamic conditions.
AI.conclusion: Both NEP and GRACE are viable MLIP frameworks for high-entropy alloys, with trade-offs between accuracy and speed. GRACE is preferred when training efficiency and accuracy are critical, while NEP is better for large-scale simulations requiring fast inference. Ensemble-based uncertainty quantification should be used over D-optimality for reliable error estimation in these systems.

title: Phonon Thermal Hall Effect in quartz and its absence in silica
summary: The observation of a misalignment between the applied heat flux and the measured temperature gradient in insulating solids induced by magnetic field has become a subject of experimental investigation, theoretical speculation, and unsettled controversy. To identify the origin of this phonon thermal Hall effect, we performed a comparative study of longitudinal and transverse heat transport in crystalline (quartz) and vitreous (silica) SiO$_2$ using identical experimental set-ups and thermometers. A finite signal was detected in the crystalline samples and none in the amorphous sample, within our resolution. The cleaner crystal exhibited a larger thermal Hall conductivity than the dirtier one, ruling out disorder as the driver of the effect. On the other hand, the amplitude of the transverse thermal resistivity is almost identical in the two crystalline samples (W$_{\perp}$/B$\approx 10^{-6}$ m.K.W$^{-1}$.T$^{-1}$). We show that in a phonon gas, as in a molecular gas displaying the Senftleben-Beenakker effect, heat is conducted through two channels, and argue that a thermal Hall response is unavoidable whenever these channels differ both in entropy production and in their coupling to the magnetic field. Under such conditions, the conserved energy current and the non-conserved entropy current cease to be parallel. Finally, the magnitude of the transverse thermal resistivity can be accounted for by a surprisingly simple picture. The heat flux induces a tiny drift velocity of the lattice nuclei, the magnetic field exerts a transverse Berry force on this drift, and this force is balanced by an entropic restoring force.

AI.tldr: Experimental study investigating the phonon thermal Hall effect in crystalline and vitreous SiO2. Results show the effect occurs only in crystalline materials, scales with crystal quality (not disorder), and can be explained by a two-channel heat conduction mechanism analogous to the Senftleben-Beenakker effect in molecular gases.
AI.motivation: To resolve the controversy around the origin of the phonon thermal Hall effect - the misalignment between applied heat flux and measured temperature gradient in insulating solids induced by magnetic field.
AI.method: Comparative study of longitudinal and transverse heat transport in crystalline (quartz) and vitreous (silica) SiO2 using identical experimental set-ups and thermometers. Detection of finite thermal Hall signal in crystals but not in amorphous sample.
AI.result: Finite thermal Hall signal detected in crystalline samples but none in amorphous sample (within resolution). Cleaner crystal exhibited larger thermal Hall conductivity than dirtier one, ruling out disorder as the driver. Transverse thermal resistivity similar in both crystalline samples (W⊥/B ≈ 10^-6 m.K.W^-1.T^-1).
AI.conclusion: The thermal Hall response arises from two-channel heat conduction in phonon gases, where energy and entropy currents become non-parallel. The transverse thermal resistivity can be explained by a drift velocity of lattice nuclei induced by heat flux, a transverse Berry force from the magnetic field, balanced by an entropic restoring force.

title: Electrochemical doping in H-terminated diamond films: Impact of O-functionalization and insights from in-situ Raman spectro electrochemistry
summary: The p-type surface conductivity of H-terminated diamond (HD, H-diamond) has created new path ways for developing diamond based electronic devices as well as chemical and bio-sensors. However, the hydrophobic nature of the HD surface can negatively impact device performance due to its low wettability. Herein, we report the study on polymer electrolyte-gated field effect transistors (EGFETs) fabricated using pristine and partially O-terminated HD films. The HD surface is transformed from hydrophobic to moderate hydrophilic by partial O-termination. Also, the sheet resistance of the HD surface increases from 7.6 to 18.7 k-Ohms per sq. while the sheet hole density decreases from 10.5 to 4.8 x 10^12 cm^-2 upon partial O-termination. Consequently, the ON - OFF ratio of the EGFET devices decreases from ~ 40 to 14 and the maximum transconductance declines from of -150 to -7.9 micro-seimens per V, but the areal capacitance increases from ~ 7.8 to 27.1 microFarad per cm^2 with partial ozonation on HD surface. In addition the in situ Raman measurements in HD EGFET provide direct experimental evidence of a gating-induced blue shift and linewidth broadening of the diamond Raman band which are associated with strong electron phonon coupling. This work highlights the significant impact of the partial O-termination on the performance of the HD EGFET devices and effect of electrochemical gating on the phonon behaviour of the H-diamond.

AI.tldr: This study investigates polymer electrolyte-gated field effect transistors (EGFETs) made from pristine and partially O-terminated H-diamond (HD) films, examining how surface termination affects device performance and phonon behavior.
AI.motivation: The hydrophobic nature of H-terminated diamond (HD) negatively impacts device performance, necessitating surface modification through partial O-termination to improve wettability while understanding its effects on electronic properties.
AI.method: Fabricated EGFET devices using pristine and partially O-terminated HD films; characterized surface properties (wettability, sheet resistance, hole density); measured device performance (ON-OFF ratio, transconductance, areal capacitance); performed in situ Raman measurements to study gating-induced phonon behavior.
AI.result: Partial O-termination transforms HD surface from hydrophobic to moderately hydrophilic; increases sheet resistance from 7.6 to 18.7 kΩ/sq; decreases sheet hole density from 10.5 to 4.8 × 10^12 cm^-2; reduces ON-OFF ratio from ~40 to 14; decreases maximum transconductance from -150 to -7.9 μS/V; increases areal capacitance from ~7.8 to 27.1 μF/cm^2; Raman shows gating-induced blue shift and linewidth broadening of diamond Raman band.
AI.conclusion: Partial O-termination significantly impacts HD EGFET performance, reducing electronic performance but increasing areal capacitance; electrochemical gating affects phonon behavior through strong electron-phonon coupling in H-diamond.

title: Altermagnetism and Room-Temperature Metal-to-Insulator Transition in CsCr$_2$S$_2$O
summary: Metal-to-insulator transitions (MITs), particularly near room temperature, have been extensively studied in nonmagnetic and conventional ferromagnetic and antiferromagnetic systems, yet the co-emergence of MIT and altermagnetism (AM) remains unexplored. Here, a layered chromium-based compound CsCr$_2$S$_2$O that realizes this coexistence was synthesized. It crystalizes in CeCr$_2$Si$_2$C-type structure with Cr moments orders in a C-type antiferromagnetic configuration below $T_\mathrm{N}$ = 326 K, constituting a room-temperature d-wave altermagnet. In the altermagnetic state, a subsequent Verwey-type MIT appears at $T_\mathrm{MI}$ = 305 K, driven by a tetragonal-to-orthorhombic structural distortion and stripe charge ordering of Cr$^{+2}$/Cr$^{+3}$ ions, while maintaining its altermagnetic character. First-principles calculations show moment-dependent spin-split electronic structures with maximum splitting energies of ~0.6 eV and ~0.3 eV in the metallic and insulating states, respectively. Our work links the two prominent phenomena, MIT and AM, in a single material, establishing a new platform for potential spintronic applications.

AI.tldr: Discovery of CsCr₂S₂O, a layered chromium-based compound that exhibits both metal-to-insulator transition (MIT) and altermagnetism (AM) near room temperature, linking two prominent phenomena in a single material for potential spintronic applications.
AI.motivation: Metal-to-insulator transitions (MITs) near room temperature have been extensively studied in nonmagnetic and conventional ferromagnetic/antiferromagnetic systems, but the co-emergence of MIT and altermagnetism (AM) remained unexplored. This work aims to discover a material that combines both phenomena.
AI.method: Synthesis of layered chromium-based compound CsCr₂S₂O with CeCr₂Si₂C-type structure; characterized using structural analysis, magnetic measurements, and first-principles calculations to study spin-split electronic structures.
AI.result: CsCr₂S₂O crystallizes in CeCr₂Si₂C-type structure with Cr moments ordering in C-type antiferromagnetic configuration below T_N = 326 K, constituting a room-temperature d-wave altermagnet. A subsequent Verwey-type MIT appears at T_MI = 305 K, driven by tetragonal-to-orthorhombic structural distortion and stripe charge ordering of Cr⁺²/Cr⁺³ ions, while maintaining altermagnetic character. First-principles calculations show maximum spin-splitting energies of ~0.6 eV (metallic state) and ~0.3 eV (insulating state).
AI.conclusion: This work successfully links MIT and AM in a single material, establishing a new platform for potential spintronic applications. The coexistence of both phenomena near room temperature in CsCr₂S₂O opens avenues for exploring novel functionalities and device applications.

title: Understanding ultrafast x-ray 'echoes' diffracted from single crystals
summary: Multiple x-ray beams generated by interference processes in perfect crystals were imaged with a resolution of about 100nm using tele-ptychography in the diffraction direction. These multiple wave-fields, also known as x-ray diffraction echoes, are related to the process known as the Pendelloesung effect and are described by dynamical diffraction theory. The echoes are produced by the constructive interference of diffracted x-rays at the exit surface of the crystal sample. In the imaged diffraction peak, we observed 10 echoes maxima with a total signal length of 78 um. Which translates into a total temporal delay in the signal of less than 108 fs.This makes the echoes of high importance for x-ray optics at x-ray Free Electron Laser sources, as the effect could be used for future ultrafast x-ray beam splitters. In addition to this application, echoes can be exploited to follow ultrafast processes in single crystal micro-structures such as melting or strain propagation.

AI.tldr: Multiple x-ray diffraction echoes (Pendelloesung effect) in perfect crystals were imaged using tele-ptychography with ~100nm resolution, revealing 10 echo maxima spanning 78um with less than 108 fs temporal delay, demonstrating potential for XFEL beam splitting applications.
AI.motivation: X-ray diffraction echoes offer a promising approach for developing ultrafast x-ray beam splitters at X-ray Free Electron Laser (XFEL) sources and enabling visualization of ultrafast processes in single crystal micro-structures such as melting or strain propagation.
AI.method: Tele-ptychography imaging in the diffraction direction with approximately 100nm resolution was used to image multiple x-ray beams generated by interference processes in perfect crystals, where echoes are produced by constructive interference of diffracted x-rays at the crystal exit surface.
AI.result: 10 echo maxima were observed in the imaged diffraction peak with a total signal length of 78 μm, translating to a total temporal delay in the signal of less than 108 femtoseconds.
AI.conclusion: X-ray diffraction echoes are of high importance for x-ray optics at XFEL sources, with potential applications for future ultrafast x-ray beam splitters and exploiting echoes to follow ultrafast processes in single crystal micro-structures including melting or strain propagation.

title: Elastic softening and fracture in randomly perforated solids
summary: We study the mechanical response of quasi-brittle polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA) specimens containing controlled random distributions of laser-cut holes. Tensile tests combined with digital image correlation reveal a nearly linear decrease of the Young's modulus with porosity, but with a softening rate far exceeding classical effective medium theory and the Hashin-Shtrikman bound. The extrapolated critical porosity at which the modulus vanishes is well below the 2D percolation threshold, indicating that ideal cylindrical void models fail to capture the observed behavior. Microscopy shows irregular pore geometries and frequent coalescence, which effectively act as crack-like defects and strongly enhance compliance. The rupture stress distributions are well described by a Weibull model accounting for both load-bearing area reduction and stress concentration at hole edges. Digital image correlation reveals heterogeneous but non-localized deformation, with strain increasingly correlated with the hole pattern, indicating a growing influence of defect-induced stress concentrations. These results highlight the dominant role of defect morphology in governing stiffness degradation and fracture statistics in porous quasi-brittle materials.

AI.tldr: Experimental study of PMMA with laser-cut holes reveals that defect morphology (irregular shapes, coalescence) rather than porosity alone governs stiffness degradation and fracture, exceeding predictions from classical theories.
AI.motivation: To understand why classical effective medium theories (Hashin-Shtrikman bound) fail to predict the mechanical response of porous quasi-brittle materials.
AI.method: Tensile tests combined with digital image correlation (DIC), microscopy analysis, and Weibull statistical modeling of rupture stress.
AI.result: Young's modulus decreases nearly linearly with porosity but at a rate far exceeding theoretical bounds; the critical porosity for zero modulus is well below 2D percolation threshold; strain becomes increasingly correlated with hole pattern; rupture stress follows Weibull distribution accounting for load-bearing area reduction and stress concentration.
AI.conclusion: Defect morphology (irregular pore geometries and coalescence) dominates stiffness degradation and fracture statistics in porous quasi-brittle materials, exceeding predictions from classical effective medium theories and ideal cylindrical void models.

title: Switching between Antiferromagnetic and Ferromagnetic Skyrmions in Two-Dimensional Magnets
summary: Antiferromagnetic (AFM) and ferromagnetic (FM) skyrmions possess unique advantages for spintronic applications. AFM skyrmions eliminate the skyrmion Hall effect and exhibit fast dynamics, whereas FM skyrmions are easier to nucleate and manipulate. However, realizing a transition between AFM and FM skyrmions within the same two-dimensional (2D) material has remained elusive. Here, using first-principles calculations and atomistic spin simulations on the Janus monolayer Cr2Ge2Te3S3, we demonstrate that strain-driven modulation of magnetic interactions enables switching between AFM and FM skyrmion phases. A compressive strain of $-3\%$ induces an AFM ground state hosting AFM skyrmions, while a tensile strain of $+2\%$ drives the system into a FM skyrmion phase.
