Moreover, under an out-of-plane magnetic field, FM skyrmions are rapidly transformed into a uniform FM phase, while AFM skyrmions transform into AFM bimerons under stronger fields. These findings establish a framework for controllable transitions between topological magnetic states in a single 2D material.

AI.tldr: Strain-driven switching between antiferromagnetic and ferromagnetic skyrmions in Janus monolayer Cr2Ge2Te3S3, enabling controllable transitions between topological magnetic states in a single 2D material.
AI.motivation: AFM and FM skyrmions each have unique advantages for spintronic applications - AFM skyrmions eliminate the skyrmion Hall effect with fast dynamics, while FM skyrmions are easier to nucleate and manipulate. However, realizing a transition between these two phases within the same 2D material has remained elusive.
AI.method: First-principles calculations and atomistic spin simulations on Janus monolayer Cr2Ge2Te3S3.
AI.result: Compressive strain of -3% induces an AFM ground state hosting AFM skyrmions, while tensile strain of +2% drives the system into an FM skyrmion phase. Under out-of-plane magnetic field, FM skyrmions rapidly transform into a uniform FM phase, while AFM skyrmions transform into AFM bimerons under stronger fields.
AI.conclusion: This work establishes a framework for controllable transitions between topological magnetic states in a single 2D material through strain engineering.

title: Atomistic theory of the phonon angular momentum Hall effect
summary: The spin and orbital Hall effects convert longitudinal charge currents into transverse flows of electronic angular momentum. Here we develop an atomistic theory of the recently proposed lattice-vibrational analogue, in which a longitudinal heat current driven by a thermal gradient is converted into a transverse current of phonon angular momentum. We derive a microscopic real-space expression for this current and show that it originates from thermally induced mixing of polarized vibrational motion, leading to a characteristic edge accumulation of phonon angular momentum. We demonstrate the effect in minimal square- and honeycomb-lattice models and compute the resulting phonon angular momentum accumulations for a range of example materials using input from first-principles calculations. Our results confirm that the phonon angular momentum Hall effect is a universal response of crystalline solids and our framework is generically applicable to all materials.

AI.tldr: The paper develops an atomistic theory for the phonon angular momentum Hall effect - the lattice-vibrational analogue of spin/orbital Hall effects where thermal gradients convert heat currents into transverse phonon angular momentum currents. The effect is demonstrated in lattice models and first-principles calculations, confirming it as a universal response in crystalline solids.
AI.motivation: Extending the spin and orbital Hall effects (converting longitudinal charge currents into transverse flows of electronic angular momentum) to their lattice-vibrational analogue, where a longitudinal heat current driven by thermal gradient converts into transverse current of phonon angular momentum.
AI.method: Development of an atomistic theory with a microscopic real-space expression for the current, derived from thermally induced mixing of polarized vibrational motion, demonstrated in square- and honeycomb-lattice models with first-principles calculations.
AI.result: Derived microscopic real-space expression for phonon angular momentum current originating from thermally induced mixing of polarized vibrational motion, leading to characteristic edge accumulation. Demonstrated in square- and honeycomb-lattice models with computed phonon angular momentum accumulations for range of example materials.
AI.conclusion: The phonon angular momentum Hall effect is confirmed as a universal response of crystalline solids, and the developed framework is generically applicable to all materials.

title: Lead-free antiperovskite derivatives Ba$_3$MA$_3$ (M = P, As, Sb, Bi; A = Cl, Br, I): Next-gen materials for optoelectronics
summary: Antiperovskite derivatives have recently emerged as promising lead-free alternatives to halide perovskites for optoelectronic applications. Here, using a comprehensive first-principles calculations including density functional perturbation theory and many-body perturbation theory (involving GW and Bethe-Salpeter equation (BSE)), we investigate the stability, excitonic, polaronic, and optoelectronic properties of cubic Ba$_3$MA$_3$ (M = P, As, Sb, Bi; A = Cl, Br, I). These compounds are found to be dynamically and thermodynamically stable direct-gap semiconductors with G$_0$W$_0$@PBE+SOC band gaps spanning 1.23-2.17 eV. BSE calculations reveal moderate exciton binding energies (0.254-0.352 eV) and intermediate-radius excitons, while Fröhlich polaron analysis indicates intermediate carrier-phonon coupling and mobilities up to $\sim$ 75 cm$^{2}$V$^{-1}$s$^{-1}$. The resulting spectroscopic limited maximum efficiencies reach $\sim$ 19-32%, surpassing several lead-based perovskites. Our results establish Ba-based antiperovskite derivatives as a robust, eco-friendly platform for next-generation optoelectronic devices.

AI.tldr: This study investigates Ba-based antiperovskite derivatives (Ba3MA3, M=P,As,Sb,Bi; A=Cl,Br,I) as lead-free alternatives to halide perovskites for optoelectronic applications using first-principles calculations.
AI.motivation: Halide perovskites are toxic (lead-based) and unstable, necessitating eco-friendly alternatives. Antiperovskites emerge as promising lead-free candidates for next-generation optoelectronic devices.
AI.method: Comprehensive first-principles calculations using density functional perturbation theory (DFPT) and many-body perturbation theory (GW and Bethe-Salpeter equation) to investigate stability, excitonic, polaronic, and optoelectronic properties.
AI.result: Ba3MA3 compounds are dynamically and thermodynamically stable direct-gap semiconductors with G0W0@PBE+SOC band gaps of 1.23-2.17 eV. Moderate exciton binding energies (0.254-0.352 eV), intermediate-radius excitons, carrier-phonon coupling with mobilities up to ~75 cm^2V^-1s^-1, and spectroscopic limited maximum efficiencies of ~19-32%.
AI.conclusion: Ba-based antiperovskite derivatives represent a robust, eco-friendly platform for next-generation optoelectronic devices, achieving efficiencies surpassing several lead-based perovskites.

title: Terahertz optical activity near crystal field transitions of Tm3+ ions in magnetoelectric alumoborates
summary: Crystal field (CF) excitations in the ground multiplet $^3H_6$ of Tm$^{3+}$ ions were investigated using terahertz transmission spectra of magnetoelectric TmAl$_3$(BO$_3$)$_4$ and Tm$_{0.05}$Yb$_{0.1}$Y$_{0.85}$Al$_3$(BO$_3$)$_4$. These excitations were identified as mainly magnetic dipole transitions from the ground singlet A$_1$ to the next excited doublet E, split by the crystal field of the D$_3$ symmetry. The fine structure of the modes was resolved at low temperatures. It manifested differently in lightly doped and in pure Tm borates, consistent with different distortions of the local crystal field with the D$_3$ symmetry. Strong natural optical activity was observed near the CF transitions resulting in a polarization plane rotation up to 25 degrees. The optical activity is quantitatively described by contributions of magnetic and electric dipole transitions to dynamic magnetoelectric susceptibility and taking into account the classification of local distortions.

AI.tldr: Crystal field excitations in Tm³⁺ ions were investigated using terahertz spectroscopy, revealing magnetic dipole transitions and strong optical activity (up to 25° rotation) in TmAl₃(BO₃)₄ borates, with different behavior in pure vs doped samples due to local crystal field distortions.
AI.motivation: Understanding crystal field (CF) excitations and their optical properties in magnetoelectric materials with rare-earth ions, particularly the relationship between local symmetry distortions and natural optical activity.
AI.method: Terahertz transmission spectroscopy was used to investigate crystal field excitations in the ground multiplet ^3H_6 of Tm³⁺ ions. Measurements were performed on magnetoelectric TmAl₃(BO₃)₄ and lightly doped Tm₀.₀₅Yb₀.₁Y₀.₈₅Al₃(BO₃)₄ at low temperatures.
AI.result: CF excitations were identified as magnetic dipole transitions from ground singlet A₁ to excited doublet E, split by crystal field of D₃ symmetry. Fine structure was resolved, showing different behavior in pure vs doped Tm borates due to different local CF distortions. Strong natural optical activity was observed near CF transitions with polarization plane rotation up to 25 degrees.
AI.conclusion: The optical activity is quantitatively described by contributions of magnetic and electric dipole transitions to dynamic magnetoelectric susceptibility, accounting for local distortions in the D₃ symmetry crystal field.

title: AlloyVAE: A generative model for complex probabilistic field-to-field relationships in alloys
summary: The inherent compositional heterogeneity of multi-principal element alloys (MPEAs) gives rise to complex, spatially varying mechanical fields that cannot be uniquely determined from coarse-grained composition descriptors. This non-uniqueness introduces intrinsically probabilistic structure-property relationships, posing a fundamental challenge to conventional deterministic modeling and machine learning approaches that collapse such mappings into average predictions. Here, we present AlloyVAE, a physics-informed generative framework that learns the full conditional distribution of mechanical fields from microstructural inputs. Built upon a conditional variational autoencoder architecture, the model incorporates learned smoothing operators to enhance functional regularity and a self-consistency mechanism to enforce physical plausibility. Trained on atomistic simulation data, AlloyVAE accurately predicts distributions of residual stress fields from composition and short-range order, and enables the generation of multiple physically consistent realizations under identical input conditions. Beyond forward prediction, the framework supports inverse design by optimizing composition fields to achieve targeted mechanical responses, and is extensible to coupled mappings involving eigenstrain. By capturing one-to-many structure-property relationships in heterogeneous materials, this work establishes a probabilistic paradigm for materials modeling and design, providing a scalable alternative to conventional simulations for navigating high-dimensional compositional spaces.

AI.tldr: AlloyVAE is a physics-informed generative framework using conditional variational autoencoders to learn probabilistic structure-property relationships in multi-principal element alloys. It predicts distributions of mechanical fields from compositional inputs, enables multiple physically consistent realizations, and supports inverse design for targeted mechanical responses.
AI.motivation: Multi-principal element alloys have inherent compositional heterogeneity causing complex, spatially varying mechanical fields that cannot be uniquely determined from coarse-grained composition descriptors. This non-uniqueness creates intrinsically probabilistic structure-property relationships, challenging conventional deterministic modeling and machine learning approaches that produce only average predictions.
AI.method: AlloyVAE uses a conditional variational autoencoder architecture with learned smoothing operators to enhance functional regularity and a self-consistency mechanism to enforce physical plausibility. Trained on atomistic simulation data, it learns the full conditional distribution of mechanical fields from microstructural inputs including composition and short-range order.
AI.result: AlloyVAE accurately predicts distributions of residual stress fields from composition and short-range order, generates multiple physically consistent realizations under identical input conditions, and enables inverse design by optimizing composition fields to achieve targeted mechanical responses. It is extensible to coupled mappings involving eigenstrain.
AI.conclusion: This work establishes a probabilistic paradigm for materials modeling and design by capturing one-to-many structure-property relationships in heterogeneous materials. It provides a scalable alternative to conventional simulations for navigating high-dimensional compositional spaces.

title: Loop-level surrogate modeling of dopant-distribution effects in Ba(Zr,Ti)O$_3$
summary: Barium titanate-based perovskites are important candidates for lead-free dielectric and electromechanical technologies. In Zr-substituted BaTiO$_3$ (BZT), functional behavior is usually discussed in terms of the average Zr concentration, while the influence of dopant spatial distribution beyond average concentration is less understood and difficult to explore systematically. Here we present an accelerated materials-design workflow that links controlled dopant distributions to full field-driven response curves. We generate a broad set of Zr distributions spanning a continuum of nanoscale arrangements, with layers, rods, dots, and lamellae serving as representative end-member motifs, and encode each configuration using a compact, parametrized descriptor model. Effective-Hamiltonian molecular dynamics is used to compute polarization-electric-field and strain-field hysteresis loops, and we train a conditional autoencoder surrogate to predict complete loops directly from the distribution parameters. This surrogate enables rapid screening and dense, property-selective design maps at scales that are not feasible with direct simulations alone, and it supports targeted follow-up simulations in regions of interest. Using the predicted loop database, we screen the distribution space for multiple functional targets, including energy-storage performance, electromechanical response, and switching behavior, and identify the corresponding dopant distribution motif families. The resulting design maps show that dopant distribution is an independent tuning parameter that can strongly affect hysteresis behavior and loop-derived figures of merit: layer-like motifs, vertical lamellae, and nanoplate-like inclusions emerge in different performance regimes. More generally, predicting full response curves enables screening of other loop-derived targets and multi-objective design in substituted ferroelectrics.

AI.tldr: This paper presents an accelerated materials-design workflow for Zr-substituted BaTiO3 that combines effective-Hamiltonian molecular dynamics with a conditional autoencoder surrogate model to predict hysteresis loops from dopant distribution parameters. The approach enables rapid screening and dense design maps, demonstrating that dopant distribution is an independent tuning parameter that strongly affects functional properties.
AI.motivation: In Zr-substituted BaTiO3 (BZT), functional behavior is typically discussed in terms of average Zr concentration, while the influence of dopant spatial distribution beyond average concentration is less understood and difficult to explore systematically.
AI.method: Accelerated materials-design workflow: (1) Generate broad set of Zr distributions with layers, rods, dots, and lamellae motifs; (2) Encode configurations using parametrized descriptor model; (3) Use effective-Hamiltonian molecular dynamics to compute polarization-electric-field and strain-field hysteresis loops; (4) Train conditional autoencoder surrogate to predict complete loops from distribution parameters.
AI.result: Design maps were generated showing dopant distribution effects on multiple functional targets: energy-storage performance, electromechanical response, and switching behavior. Different performance regimes correspond to different dopant distribution motif families (layer-like, vertical lamellae, nanoplate-like inclusions).
AI.conclusion: Dopant distribution is confirmed as an independent tuning parameter that strongly affects hysteresis behavior and loop-derived figures of merit. Different motifs (layer-like, vertical lamellae, nanoplate-like inclusions) emerge in different performance regimes. This approach enables multi-objective design and screening of loop-derived targets in substituted ferroelectrics.
